coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots and AuNCs co-doped electrospun membranes for ratiometric fluorescent determination of cyanide
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Yan
Lu, Xiaomei
Jiang, Xiaoming
Wu, Peng
coredata.dc:description:
Due to the extremely toxic nature of cyanide, the World Health Organization (WHO) has set 1.9 μM as the maximum acceptable level in drinking water. Therefore, methods for facile screening and on-site detection of cyanide is urgently needed. In this work, a ratiometric fluorescent method for cyanide detection was developed based on blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) and red-emitting AuNCs. To suit an on-site detection, the two fluorophores were incorporated into a nanofibrous membrane through electrospinning, yielding the core-shell structure of CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA. Upon reaction with cyanide, the red fluorescence of AuNCs was quenched but the blue fluorescence of CDs was retained, resulting in ratiometric fluorescence change. Besides, both CDs and AuNCs can be excited with a handheld UV lamp (365 nm), and the resultant visible fluorescence color change could be visualized with naked eye. The proposed ratiometric method provided a limit of detection (LOD) 0.15 μM for cyanide, which was far lower than 1.9 μM. The membrane was further employed for screening analysis of cyanide in tap water samples. After collection of 500 analytical results, only 5.6% of the results located outside the
X ¯
± 2 s criteria, while for the
X ¯
± 3 s
criteria, there is no outlier. The statistical results indicated that the proposed method may be potentially useful for screening analysis of cyanide in water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cyanide
Ratiometric fluorescent detection
Carbon dots
AuNCs
Electrospinning

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Spectroscopic characterization of the ratiometric fluorescent detection
Morphological analysis of the electrospun NFM
Mechanism of the fluorescence quenching of AuNCs by cyanide
High selectivity and anti-interference of NFM
Determination of cyanide in tap water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology of the People's Republic of China
Department of Science and Technology of Sichuan Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Spectroscopic characterization of the CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA NFM for ratiometric fluorescent detection of cyanide (5 μM, 5 μL): (A) CDs-PVA@CA NFM; (B) AuNCs-PVA@CA NFM; and (C) CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA NFM. The insets show the corresponding fluorescent images excited with daylight and a handheld 365 nm UV lamp.
SEM images of NFMs: (A) and (D) CA; (B) and (E) CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA; and (C) and (F) CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA with cyanide (10 μM, 5 μL).

Characterization of the interaction between NFM and cyanide: (A) UV–vis spectra of AuNCs before and after adding CN−; (B) Fluorescence lifetime of AuNCs before and after adding CN−; and (C) XPS spectra of Au 4f for the CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA NFM before and after interaction with CN− (2 mM).

Selectivity evaluation of the CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA NFM towards cyanide (20 μM) over common anions (A) and metal cations (B). The insets show the corresponding fluorescent images excited with daylight and a handheld 365 nm UV lamp. 10 μM for all the common anions and metal ions.

Interference study about the ratiometric fluorescent detection of CN−: (A) the response of the ratiometric visual detection for CN−, Ag+, Co2+, Fe3+, Ni2+ and Pb2+ in the presence of EDTA/GSH (both 1 mM); and (B) corresponding fluorescence images excited with daylight and a handheld 365 nm UV lamp. 500 μM for all the metal ions (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

Detection of cyanide by CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA NFM: (A) fluorescence spectra of the ratiometric detection; (B) the corresponding calibration curve for the detection of cyanide; and (C) optical images of the NFMs excited with daylight and a handheld 365 nm UV lamp (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

Results obtained in the analysis of tap water samples spiked with 1.9 μM cyanide with the proposed CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA NFM. 100 tap water sample replicates were carried out. The red line represents the average value of the analysis (2.03 μM), while the blue and organge broken lines represent
X ¯
± s and
X ¯
± 2 s , respectivley (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
Schematic illustration of the preparation of the ratiometric detection (CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA) and the fluorescence quenching mechanism of AuNCs by cyanide.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots and AuNCs co-doped electrospun membranes for ratiometric fluorescent determination of cyanide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials and Environmental Protection, Chengdu Textile College, Chengdu, Sichuan 611731, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials and Environmental Protection

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials and Environmental Protection, Chengdu Textile College, Chengdu, Sichuan 611731, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical & Testing Center, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610064, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analytical & Testing Center, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610064, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Due to the extremely toxic nature of cyanide, the World Health Organization (WHO) has set 1.9 μM as the maximum acceptable level in drinking water. Therefore, methods for facile screening and on-site detection of cyanide is urgently needed. In this work, a ratiometric fluorescent method for cyanide detection was developed based on blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) and red-emitting AuNCs. To suit an on-site detection, the two fluorophores were incorporated into a nanofibrous membrane through electrospinning, yielding the core-shell structure of CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA. Upon reaction with cyanide, the red fluorescence of AuNCs was quenched but the blue fluorescence of CDs was retained, resulting in ratiometric fluorescence change. Besides, both CDs and AuNCs can be excited with a handheld UV lamp (365 nm), and the resultant visible fluorescence color change could be visualized with naked eye. The proposed ratiometric method provided a limit of detection (LOD) 0.15 μM for cyanide, which was far lower than 1.9 μM. The membrane was further employed for screening analysis of cyanide in tap water samples. After collection of 500 analytical results, only 5.6% of the results located outside the
X ¯
± 2 s criteria, while for the
X ¯
± 3 s
criteria, there is no outlier. The statistical results indicated that the proposed method may be potentially useful for screening analysis of cyanide in water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Cyanide (CN−) is one of the most toxic ions and is extremely harmful to living beings (Dash et al., 2009; L.F. Zhang et al., 2019). According to the World Health Organization (WHO), the maximum acceptable level of cyanide in drinking water is only 1.9 μM (World Health Organization, 1996). In view of its toxicity and extensive applications in industries, the development of sensitive and convenient methods for detection of trace amounts of cyanide is of great significance (Wang et al., 2014). Traditionally, approaches such as spectrophotometry (Noroozifar et al., 2005; Lin et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2019) and electrochemical methodology (Shan et al., 2004) are used for determination of cyanide, but suffer from the drawbacks of relative cumbersome operation and are often time-consuming. Optical sensors, such as organic dyes (Yang and Tae, 2006), supramolecular complexes (Lin et al., 2016; Lin et al., 2015; Li et al., 2019), bronsted-acid derivatives (Badugu et al., 2005) and metal-dye complexes (Yoon et al., 2012; Holaday et al., 2014), are successful for detection of cyanide. However, the synthetic endeavor adds difficulty for on-site detection. Besides, most of the reported fluorescent sensors for cyanide worked in single wavelength detection scheme, making accurate detection problematic due to interferences from analyte-independent factors (Wu et al., 2016; Lu et al., 2018). Therefore, developing ratiometric fluorescent methods that can afford on-site detection of cyanide is still urgently demanded.

Electrospinning is a promising approach for design and construction of nanofibrous membranes (NFM) for a variety of applications ranging from filtration membranes and catalytic supports to photonic and electronic devices (Xue et al., 2019; Shi et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2014). These NFMs assembled by electrospun fibers commonly feature high porosity and large specific surface area (Huang et al., 2003; Thenmozhi et al., 2017). Particularly, such features endow electrospun fibrous membranes with high adsorption and fast response for targets, making them highly suitable for construction of various optical sensors (Yang et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2002; Yang et al., 2018; Zeng et al., 2016). Moreover, due to the ease in post handling (such as cutting off, transportation, and storage), the resultant membrane is appealing for on-site analysis. However, up to date, no report on the use of electrospun NFM for detection of cyanide has emerged.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic characterization of the ratiometric fluorescent detection
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the CDs and AuNCs co-doped NFM, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) aqueous solution was taken as the matrix for solubilizing the above two fluorescent components. As shown in Scheme 1, cellulose acetate (CA) fiber was firstly electrospun for 30 min (organic phase, N, N-dimethyl acetamide/acetone = 1:2). The thin layer of CA on the receiver acted as the substrate for subsequent collection of the fibers from PVA spinning solution containing CDs and AuNCs. Afterwards, another layer of CA (electrospun for 30 min again) was covered onto the surface of PVA layer for protection of CDs and AuNCs from leaking out into water.

First, the fluorescent property of the CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA NFM was investigated for evidencing the successful doping of CDs and AuNCs. As shown in Fig. S2, the CDs obtained from hydrothermal treating of citrate-ethylenediamine mixture featured an emission maximum at 445 nm upon excitation at 365 nm, which is similar to the previous reports (Zhu et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019). Upon doping into PVA@CA, the fluorescence was retained, yielding blue-emitting membrane (Fig. 1 A). Similarly, red-emitting (˜650 nm) membrane was obtained after doping the well-known BSA-AuNCs (Xie et al., 2009; Xiong et al., 2019) into PVA@CA (Fig. 1B). Therefore, individual CDs and AuNCs could be successfully doped into the PVA@CA NFM. Obviously, the emission spectra of CDs and AuNCs are well-resolved. Besides, the excitation spectra of CDs and AuNCs contain considerable overlap (Fig. S3), allowing simultaneous excitation for potential ratiometric investigation.

In the fluorescence spectrum of CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA membrane, two characteristic emission bands at 445 and 650 nm were observed upon excitation at 365 nm (Fig. 1C). According to the above fluorescent characterizations, such two bands could be identified as CDs and AuNCs, respectively. Besides, due to the color mixing, pink fluorescence from the membrane was received. Therefore, co-doping of CDs and AuNCs into the NFM was successful.

Next, the response of the resultant membrane to cyanide was investigated. As can be evidenced from Fig. 1A, no fluorescence change was observed upon mixing of cyanide (5 μM) with CDs-PVA@CA membrane, but obvious fluorescence quenching was received by AuNCs-PVA@CA membrane (Fig. 1B). For CDs and AuNCs co-doped membrane, the original fluorescence response of CDs and AuNCs to cyanide was retained (Fig. 1C), i.e., the 650 band (AuNCs) quenched considerably but the 450 band remained almost unchanged. In other words, ratiometric response was obtained. For the fluorescent photos, the CDs and AuNCs co-doped membrane changed from pink to bluish pink, yielding better visual resolution than individual AuNCs doped membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological analysis of the electrospun NFM
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the NFMs was characterized with scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Fig. 2 ) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Fig. S4). As shown in Fig. 2, all the composite materials exhibited fine nanofibrous morphologies and networked microstructures, providing the material basis for potentially excellent sensing performance. Besides, all the fibers are randomly oriented with a wide fiber diameter distribution (120–460 nm, measured with the Nano Measure software). Significantly, no obvious difference could be identified between pure CA NFM and CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA NFM, indicating that doping of CDs/AuNCs caused minimal change to the structures of CA NFM. Moreover, after treating the CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA NFM with cyanide, the fiber distribution became dense and the diameter was more uniform (average 260 nm), indicating a potential reaction between cyanide with AuNCs. Meanwhile, similar sign of reaction can also be observed from the corresponding TEM images (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the fluorescence quenching of AuNCs by cyanide
ce:section.ce:para:
In view of the structure of AuNCs, it is well-known that BSA-AuNCs contains both Au(0) core and a small amount of Au(I) shelled on the surface (as can be seen from the XPS of AuNCs in Fig. 3 C) (Xie et al., 2009). Both the Au(0) core and the Au(I) shell are indispensable for the fluorescence of AuNCs. Therefore, any partial variation of the oxidation state of the AuNCs (Au(0) or Au(I)) is expected to result in fluorescence quenching. According to Elsener reaction (theoretical) (Qing et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2009):
4Au + 8CN− + 2H2O + O2 → 4Au(CN)2 − + 4OH−
Au(0) will be oxidized into Au(I) by oxygen (air) assisted by cyanide. To see whether the Elsener reaction occurred in the proposed system, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence lifetime, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterizations were carried out. As shown in Fig. 3A, the absorption of AuNCs decreased considerably in the whole absorption range, indicating appreciable etching of AuNCs by cyanide. Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetime of AuNCs also shortened in the presence of cyanide (2.01 μs to 1.90 μs), but much less significant than that of fluorescence (Fig. 1C) and UV–vis absorption (Fig. 3A). Therefore, static quenching is dominant for cyanide-induced fluorescence quenching of AuNCs, and this agrees well with the cyanide-resultant etching.

Next, XPS was employed to quantitatively analyze the change of Au(0) content in AuNCs. As shown in Fig. 3C, two distinct peaks can be deconvoluted from the Au 4f7/2 XPS spectrum, which can be assigned to Au(0) (83.6 eV) and the Au(I)-mercaptan complex (87.3 eV) (Xie et al., 2009), with relative amounts of 56.15% and 43.84%, respectively. After interaction with cyanide, the above two peaks retained, but the Au(I) peak received a ˜0.2 eV positive shift. Besides, the relative amounts Au(0) and Au(I) changed to 40.95% and 59.05%, respectively. Obviously, upon reaction with cyanide, the relative amount of Au(0) decreased, which is consistent with the theoretical Elsener reaction. In other words, the presence of cyanide resulted in etching of AuNCs in the CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA NFM, leading to fluorescence quenching of AuNCs. Since the fluorescence of CDs was influenced minimally, the CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA NFM exhibited a ratiometric response towards cyanide.

One of the advantages of ratiometric fluorescent sensing is that besides ratiometric response for correlating the interference from analyte-independent factors, the fluorescent color change can be easily visualized via naked eye (Wu et al., 2015), which significantly improves the accuracy and resolution of visual detection. In other words, the sensitivity of such ratiometric sensor is related to the ratio of the two input fluorescent components. Therefore, the corresponding ratios of CDs and AuNCs for blue and red fluorescence inputs in CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA NFM were studied. Four representative fluorescence spectra and color of NFM prepared at CDs:AuNCs mass ratio of 1:10, 1:50, 1:80 and 1:100 were collected. As shown in Fig. S5, in the presence of cyanide (1.9 μM), the color of the NFM prepared with CDs/AuNCs mass ratio of 1:50 exhibited the largest fluorescence intensity decrease and most obvious color change (from red to blue-purple). For the mass ratio of 1:10, although the fluorescence intensity decrease was similar to that of 1:50, the color from the background fluorescence was significant. For the mass ratio of 1:80 and 1:100, the quenching was only minimal, together with slight color change (1:100), probably due to excess amounts of AuNCs. Eventually, CDs/AuNCs mass ratio of 1:50 was employed for the NFM construction.

Next, the thickness of the CA shell was investigated. Covering a thin layer of water-insoluble CA could prevent possible leaking of CDs and AuNCs into the solution during sensing applications. However, too thick of the layer would prevent the interaction between AuNCs and cyanide, resulting in lower sensitivity. The thickness of the CA shell was controlled through the electrospinning time. As shown in Fig. S6, electrospinning time of 30 min could compromise the sensitivity and stability of the membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: High selectivity and anti-interference of NFM
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the utility of the CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA NFM in a cyanide-selective visual sensing system, the selectivity of the NFM towards a series of common anions and metal cations was investigated. For quantitative comparison, the ratiometric response (I445/I650) was explored for evaluation. As shown in Fig. 4 A, of the investigated 17 anions, including common halides and oxysalts anions, no significant response was observed except cyanide. Meanwhile, only cyanide could induce visual color change of the fluorescent membrane. Upon co-existing, the interferences were also minimal (Fig. S7A).

Next, the selectivity of the CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA NFM toward cyanide over common metal cations was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4B, alkaline and alkaline earth metal ions, Al3+, and transition metal ions of Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+ and Hg2+ exhibited little responses. Similarly, the co-existing interferences were also negligible (Fig. S7B). In the case of Ag+, Co2+, Fe3+, Ni2+ and Pb2+, however, decreased ratiometric responses were observed (Fig. 4B). It should be noted that in the previous work of using AuNCs solution for cyanide detection (Liu et al., 2010), besides the above Ag+, Co2+, Fe3+, Ni2+ and Pb2+, interferences from Zn2+, Cu2+, Cd2+ and Hg2+ were also observed. Therefore, doping of AuNCs into the NFM increased the selectivity considerably.

To decrease the potential influence of Ag+, Co2+, Fe3+, Ni2+ and Pb2+ on cyanide detection, here we proposed the use of EDTA/GSH for masking. EDTA is known to coordinate with a broadband of metal cations, while GSH can bind with a lot of heavy metal ions. The combination of EDTA and GSH can thus be expected to alleviate the interactions of the above metal ions with the CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA NFM. As shown in Fig. 5 A, upon addition of EDTA/GSH (both 1 mM), the response of the NFM (in the absence of cyanide) towards these metal ions (500 μM) were considerable decreased, as compared the cases in Fig. 4B. Meanwhile, these responses were almost identical to the blank values, indicating that the original responses were efficiently masked by EDTA and GSH. When co-existed with cyanide, the ratiometric responses of the NFM were also similar to that of only cyanide (red pillar, Fig. 5A). For visual detection (Fig. 5B), the color change in the absence of cyanide was largely alleviated as compared with those presented in Fig. 4B. In the presence of cyanide, the color change for all the cases were almost the same, except slight different color from the Fe3+-coexisted sample. However, it should be noted that here the concentrations of these interfering ions were as high as 500 μM, which is higher than the typical concentrations of these ions in common water samples.

To maximize the sensitivity of the ratiometric detection of cyanide, the assay working conditions were optimized. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the fluorescence of AuNCs (@650 nm, Fig. S9) and the ratiometric response (I 445/I 650, Fig. 6B) decreased with increasing amounts of cyanide. Plotting the ratiometric fluorescence intensity with the concentration of cyanide yielded an excellent linear relationship, with linear correlation function of Y = 0.14 X + 0.13 (0.2 – 20 μM, R 2 = 0.996). The limit of detection (LOD, 3σ) was estimated to be as low as 0.15 μM, which is ∼13 times lower than the maximum level (1.9 μM) of cyanide in drinking water permitted by the WHO. In addition, fluorescent photos were taken for the above cyanide-treated CDs/AuCNs-PVA@CA NFMs with a handheld 365 nm UV lamp. As shown in Fig. 6C, gradual fluorescent color change from pink to blue could be observed upon increasing the concentration of cyanide, demonstrating potential visual and semi-quantitative detection of cyanide. Besides, the proposed assay exhibited lower or equivalent LOD over many existing methods (Table S2).

Use of ratiometric fluorescence change for visual detection of cyanide provides better color resolution over single color input. As shown in Fig. S9, using AuNCs as the single fluorescence input, the fluorescence color of the resultant AuCNs-PVA@CA NFM changed from bright red to dark, which is difficult to be differentiated from each other. Upon addition of another constant blue background from CDs (Fig. S8), the fluorescence color resolution was substantially improved (Fig. 6C), particularly at low concentration of cyanide. Therefore, the use of ratiometric fluorescence for visual detection improved the naked eye observation sensitivity for low concentrations of cyanide.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of cyanide in tap water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further assess the applicability of the developed ratiometric fluorescent detection of cyanide in real water samples, tap water samples were taken as the sample matrices. The tap water samples were first filtrated with a 0.45 μM membrane to remove suspensions and then subjected to analysis. Average spike-recoveries ranging from 95% to 105% were obtained, indicating potential usefulness of the proposed method for determination of cyanide in water samples.

Next, the performance of the ratiometric detection for screening analysis of cyanide in water samples was evaluated. The arrangement of the screening analysis is based on the protocol developed by Resano et al. to evaluate a method for screening out large number of samples that are out of the scope of interest (Resano et al., 2005). For such purpose, 100 pieces of CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA NFM were prepared and treated with tap water samples containing 1.9 μM cyanide. Eventually, 500 analytical results were obtained on different sites on the NFM. As can be seen from Fig. 7 , there is a relatively high dispersion for the individual replicates. The individual values follow a Gaussian distribution with a mean value of 2.03 μM and a relative standard deviation of 15.4%. Besides, as usual as in screening analysis (Resano et al., 2005), some outliers appear, representing 30.8% and 5.6% for the
X ¯
± s (one fold of SD) and
X ¯
± 2 s (two fold of SD), respectively. For the
X ¯
± 3 s
criteria, there is no outlier. Accordingly, potential overestimated results based on the
X ¯
± 2 s criteria were minimal. It is expected that if the replicate measurement number increases, the distribution of the results (
X ¯
± s ) will be further narrowed. Therefore, the proposed CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA NFM is potentially useful for screening analysis of water samples for trace cyanide.

In summary, a ratiometric fluorescent method for cyanide detection was developed via co-electrospinning of blue-emitting CDs and red-emitting AuNCs. The resultant CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA NFM not only possessed the unique structure and function of typical electronspun fiber membrane, but also retained the excellent optical properties of both CDs and AuNCs. In the presence of cyanide, the fluorescence of AuNCs was quenched but not CDs, resulting in ratiometric fluorescence response. The LOD for cyanide was 0.15 μM, much lower than the level set by the WHO. Besides, since both of the fluorescence of AuNCs and CDs could be excited with a handheld UV lamp, the CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA NFM could be explored for facile on-site visual analysis of cyanide with naked eye. Overall, the CDs/AuNCs-PVA@CA NFM-based test strips featured low-cost, easy fabrication, and potential usefulness in screening analysis of cyanide in water samples. Besides, since NFM is also advantageous for adsorption, it is therefore appealing to explore the AuNCs-based NFM for potential cyanide removal. Moreover, considering the appealing feature of the smartphone for on-site detection, it would be more convenient and accurate while integrating with a smartphone for color scanning (Upadhyay et al., 2018; Anand and Sahoo, 2019).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775105), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2017YFD0801203), and the Sichuan Youth Science and Technology Foundation (No. 2016JQ0019).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2019.121368.
